title: Reaffirming the freedom to decide and expressing continued support for medication abortion access.
plain_summary: This bill is about supporting people's right to get abortion medication through the mail. The House of Representatives wants to make it clear that federal law allows abortion pills to be mailed when they are being used legally. They are against using an old law called the Comstock Act to stop abortions from happening. The bill also says that people should have access to reproductive health care without unnecessary restrictions.
last_action_text: Referred to the Committee on Energy and Commerce, and in addition to the Committee on the Judiciary, for a period to be subsequently determined by the Speaker, in each case for consideration of such p

sections.title: Whereas Clauses
sections.text: People who oppose abortion are trying very hard to ban abortion everywhere in the United States. They want to use an old law called the Comstock Act to do this. For almost one hundred years, the Department of Justice, courts, and Congress have all agreed that the Comstock laws do not apply to legal abortions. Most of the public supports access to abortion, and eighty percent of Americans oppose a federal law that would ban abortion nationwide. People who oppose abortion are fighting against reproductive rights, including the right to make personal and private health care decisions. Using the Comstock Act incorrectly would be another way to attack access to medication abortion, which is a safe and effective health care option. Using the Comstock Act wrong could also threaten the shipping and transportation of basic medical materials. Using the Comstock Act wrong could further threaten and endanger sexual and reproductive health.

sections.title: Resolution
sections.text: The House of Representatives confirms the existing authority to distribute medication abortion under federal law. This was confirmed by a December twenty third, two thousand twenty two memo from the Department of Justice's Office of Legal Counsel to the General Counsel of the United States Postal Service. The memo states that Section fourteen sixty one of title eighteen of the U.S. Code does not prohibit mailing certain drugs that can be used to perform abortions when the person sending them does not intend for the recipient to use them unlawfully. The House of Representatives also condemns restrictions on access to and coverage of reproductive health care, including medication abortion like mifepristone (a drug used for medication abortion).
